item.title: The window manager rabbithole
item.description:
Desktop linux is, for all its quirks, my most comfortable development environment; really for just two primary reasons:

  • I like writing code in an environment similar to where it will execute, which makes linux easy pickings for someone doing backend web dev work
  • Your capacity to customize your development environment is a function of how masochistic you are about your configurations

The cool thing about linux desktop environments & window managers is that if you've ever had a weird idea for how you wish you could interact with your computer, chance is someone gave a shot at manifesting that idea & it has a zealous following of people who swear by it as a productivity booster.

Want an "out of the box" experience? One of the big desktop environments, like GNOME or KDE Plasma are probably right for you. Love vim keybindings and hate your mouse? A tiling WM like i3wm might be more your speed. If you're allergic to desktop icons and want to navigate through nested tree menus to launch everything, a stacking WM like openbox would suit your fancy.

Even after you've chosen how you want your window manager or desktop environment to look, you have all these sub-options each influenced by their own philosophical perspective on software. If you want zero commercial influence, something like the Qt-based KDE might not fit the bill. If continuity and backwards compatibility are your primary focus, you might be best off with a WM that doesn't iterate and, aside from bugfixes, has barely changed in years. All these factors end up generating a lot of options.

So, eventually you just pick one. On a sliding scale from "pre-packaged solution" like a big desktop environment to "configurable only through source editing & recompilation", like suckless software, I fell somewhere in the middle with i3wm. However, you're likely to notice that, depending on your choice, your desktop feels very barren. A WM installed without friends is lonely indeed, and the next step is figuring out a patchwork quilt of tools to fill the hole left by the now-absent desktop environment associated applications you once took for granted. You have no built in GNOME-calculator or KDE system monitor.

This is a double edged process. On the plus side, it's a great way of pruning your installation into a lean, mean machine, since the only things you will reinstall or seek alternatives for are things you cared about in the first place. On the other hand, you have to go through the sometimes exhausting process of finding an alternative for things you never realized people made alternatives for. There's a lot of trial and error involved in picking a new status bar, configuring it, trying to figure out why its plugins aren't working with your audio stack & won't display your current volume, researching, trying a new one, etc, then repeating the process with a file browser, a notification manager, a compositor, etc.

If you persevere though, there're some very cool things you can create that you'd have a much tougher time recreating on Windows, OSX, or in a big desktop environment. The folks at the unixporn subreddit (SFW) and their nonstop posting of terminal-rendered ASCII tools composited to have corner rounding, huge shadows, and wobbly windows or flame effects is a constant reminder that with some practice it is possible to make some very neat and comfortable looking environments.

Given that many of the tools now installed on your desktop are configured via plaintext dotfiles, there's a limitless amount of tweaking and scripting you can do. You can programmatically generate backgrounds based on your computer's most active processes, or what's trending on twitter. You can use something like pywal to match the color palette of your GTK theme to your background. As long as you're handy with bash and sed or a little bit of python, the sky's the limit.

I think there's a tendency to "go all out" and end up with a house of cards that all falls apart when some underlying dependency updates, à la xkcd 2347. However, with time it's possible to build your little suite of tools and configurations into something that I've felt is more stable than many big DEs. The best part about this sort of system is that plaintext configurations make serializing and version tracking your entire setup very simple; at the end of the day you can just shove all your configurations into a git tracked directory. What's more, is you can branch into different configurations between your laptop and desktop and tie in all sorts of deployment scripts to go from the arch linux iso installation screen to a fully featured and familiar environment in just a few minutes. That's what I have for my systems here.

All in all I'd definitely suggest at least looking down the rabbithole if you're not content with the way you interact with your computer. There's probably a WM out there for you.
